perturbation.original_text: 1. Purpose 1.1 The parties intend to provide each other with certain information of the Technology Hubs in secret and confidential nature pertaining to their business proposal and/or its operations. 1.2 The parties wish to record the terms and conditions upon which each shall disclose confidential information to the other, which terms and conditions shall constitute a binding and enforceable agreement between the parties and /or their agents /representatives . 1.3 This agreement shall also bind the parties, notwithstanding the date of signature hereof, in the event that either party shall have disclosed any confidential information to the other party prior to date of signature hereof. 1.4 For the purpose of this agreement the party which discloses confidential information shall be referred to as “the disclosing party” and the party which receives the confidential information shall be referred to as “the receiving party”.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Purpose 1.1 The parties may provide each other with certain information of the Technology Hubs, which may or may not be considered confidential, pertaining to their business proposal and/or its operations. 1.2 The parties wish to record the terms and conditions upon which each shall disclose confidential information to the other, which terms and conditions shall constitute a non-binding agreement between the parties and /or their agents /representatives . 1.3 This agreement shall also bind the parties, only from the date of signature hereof, in the event that either party shall have disclosed any confidential information to the other party. 1.4 For the purpose of this agreement the party which discloses confidential information shall sometimes be referred to as “the disclosing party” and the party which receives the confidential information shall sometimes be referred to as “the receiving party”.
perturbation.explanation: Section 1.1 changes 'intend to provide' to 'may provide,' and 'secret and confidential nature' to 'which may or may not be considered confidential'. Section 1.2 changes 'binding and enforceable agreement' to 'non-binding agreement'. Section 1.3 restricts the agreement's binding nature to only from the date of signature, removing retrospective effect. Section 1.4 changes 'shall be referred to' to 'sometimes be referred to'. This undermines the certainty and enforceability of the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Confidentiality Obligation 3.1 All confidential information made available by one party to the other shall be kept secret from third parties. 3.2 No confidential information received may be supplied to a third party without the prior written permission of the other party hereto. 3.3 Beyond this, each party also undertakes as follows: 3.3.1 not to use the confidential information of the other party for any purposes other than the purpose contemplated by this agreement; 3.3.2 not to modify or reconstruct, cause others to reconstruct or use for any purposes other than the purpose contemplated by this agreement, any object made ava ilable to it by the other party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 1 introduces ambiguity and non-binding elements, directly contradicting the Confidentiality Obligation in Section 3. Section 1.1 changes 'intend to provide' to 'may provide,' and 'secret and confidential nature' to 'which may or may not be considered confidential,' undermining the basis for confidentiality. Section 1.2 changes 'binding and enforceable agreement' to 'non-binding agreement,' making the subsequent confidentiality obligations unenforceable. The original text clearly establishes an agreement to disclose 'secret and confidential' information, with strict obligations regarding its handling. The changed text undermines this by suggesting information may not be confidential and that the agreement is non-binding, thus creating a direct contradiction with the confidentiality obligations outlined in Section 3, which mandates that 'all confidential information...shall be kept secret' and cannot be disclosed to third parties without permission.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Designation 2.1 Information, documents, software, objects and other materials to be kept secret (hereinafter: "confidential information") shall be designated as such in writing by the disclosing party. Written designation shall be in the form of an unmistakable annotation such as secret , confidential , for restricted internal access only , etc. Orally given informat ion need only be kept secret if designated as confidential information at the time of its disclosure and supplied in addition within 14 (fourteen) days to the receiving party in summarised written form marked as confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Designation 2.1 Information, documents, software, objects and other materials (hereinafter: "confidential information") may or may not be designated as such in writing by the disclosing party. Written designation may be in the form of a suggestion of an annotation such as secret , confidential , for restricted internal access only , etc. Orally given informat ion might not need to be kept secret, even if designated as confidential information at the time of its disclosure and supplied in addition within 14 (fourteen) days to the receiving party in summarised written form marked as confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Section 2.1 changes 'shall be designated' to 'may or may not be designated' and alters the requirement for written designation from mandatory to optional with 'may be in the form of a suggestion'. It also weakens the oral information protection making it 'might not need to be kept secret'. This contradicts the confidentiality obligations outlined in Section 3.1 and weakens section 5.1 and 5.2 obligations in Disclosure to Employees and Affiliated Companies.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Confidentiality Obligation 3.1 All confidential information made available by one party to the other shall be kept secret from third parties. 3.2 No confidential information received may be supplied to a third party without the prior written permission of the other party hereto. 3.3 Beyond this, each party also undertakes as follows: 3.3.1 not to use the confidential information of the other party for any purposes other than the purpose contemplated by this agreement; 3.3.2 not to modify or reconstruct, cause others to reconstruct or use for any purposes other than the purpose contemplated by this agreement, any object made ava ilable to it by the other party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 2.1 weakens the confidentiality requirements by changing 'shall be designated' to 'may or may not be designated' and altering the requirement for written designation from mandatory to optional. This directly contradicts section 3.1, which states that 'All confidential information made available by one party to the other shall be kept secret from third parties'. The change introduces ambiguity about whether information must be kept secret, which directly conflicts with the obligation in section 3.1.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Confidentiality Obligation 3.1 All confidential information made available by one party to the other shall be kept secret from third parties. 3.2 No confidential information received may be supplied to a third party without the prior written permission of the other party hereto. 3.3 Beyond this, each party also undertakes as follows: 3.3.1 not to use the confidential information of the other party for any purposes other than the purpose contemplated by this agreement; 3.3.2 not to modify or reconstruct, cause others to reconstruct or use for any purposes other than the purpose contemplated by this agreement, any object made ava ilable to it by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Confidentiality Obligation 3.1 All confidential information made available by one party to the other should ideally be kept secret from third parties. 3.2 Confidential information received may be supplied to a third party with a verbal heads up to the other party hereto. 3.3 Beyond this, each party should endeavour as follows: 3.3.1 to try not to use the confidential information of the other party for any purposes other than the purpose contemplated by this agreement; 3.3.2 to not unnecessarily modify or reconstruct, cause others to reconstruct or use for any purposes other than the purpose contemplated by this agreement, any object made ava ilable to it by the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Section 3.1 changes 'shall be kept secret' to 'should ideally be kept secret'. Section 3.2 changes 'No confidential information received may be supplied' to 'Confidential information received may be supplied' with just a 'verbal heads up'. Section 3.3 changes 'each party also undertakes' to 'each party should endeavour'. Within 3.3.1 'not to use' becomes 'to try not to use'. Finally, 3.3.2 adds 'unnecessarily' to make avoiding modification conditional. The term confidential information being modified here, also introduces an in-text contradiction regarding the Exceptions section, because now, the rule is that confidential information should only 'ideally' be kept secret.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Exceptions 4.1 The obligation to observe secrecy under this Agreement shall not apply to: 4.1.1 information, documents (including information and documents in machine -readable form), software, objects and other materials that is generally available to the public, or become generally available to the public other than as a breach of this Non -disclosur e Agreement; 4.1.2 information that is already lawfully in the possession of one of the parties hereto prior to the effective date of this Non -disclosure Agreement, without having been obtained from the other party or one of its affiliated companies; 4.1.3 information that is received by one of the parties hereto from a third party after the effective date of this Non -disclosure Agreement, provided this third party has not violated any Non -disclosure Agreement; 4.1.4 information the publication of which is required by statute and/or common law; 4.1.5 information that the receiving party, as evidenced by written records, has independently acquired or developed; Nevertheless, each party shall keep the use of the information by the other party secret from third parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the confidentiality obligations. 'shall be kept secret' is changed to 'should ideally be kept secret', 'No confidential information received may be supplied' is changed to allowing disclosure with just a 'verbal heads up'. The 'undertakes' is changed to 'should endeavour'. This creates a contradiction with Section 4, which outlines specific exceptions to the obligation to observe secrecy. By making the initial confidentiality obligation weaker and conditional, it contradicts the intention of the exception clauses, which assume a strong initial obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Disclosure to Employees and Affiliated Companies 5.1 Each party shall make available confidential information to its employees only in so far as the employees need it to fulfil the purpose as set out in 1. above. It will ensure that these employ ees are equally obliged to observe secrecy, whereby the obligation shall survive the termination of their respective employment contracts. 5.2 Disclosure of confidential information to affiliated companies is permitted under the conditions laid out in 5.1 above.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Disclosure to Employees and Affiliated Companies 5.1 Each party may consider making available confidential information to its employees if the employees might need it to fulfil the purpose as set out in 1. above. It hopes that these employ ees are somewhat obliged to observe secrecy, although the obligation might not survive the termination of their respective employment contracts. 5.2 Disclosure of confidential information to affiliated companies is suggested under similar conditions to those laid out in 5.1 above.
perturbation.explanation: Section 5.1 changes 'shall make available' to 'may consider making available', 'in so far as the employees need it' to 'if the employees might need it', 'will ensure' to 'hopes that', and 'are equally obliged' to 'are somewhat obliged', and changes 'whereby the obligation shall survive' to 'although the obligation might not survive'. Section 5.2 changes 'is permitted' to 'is suggested'. This creates doubt about whether confidentiality will actually be maintained or not. The new suggested form to keep confidential information, contradicts any legal action, since it's suggesting to keep confidential, but in case that doesn't happen it will be fine
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. Liabilities 9.1 Any claim for damages to be limited to actual damages. The parties shall not be liable for any consequential or indirect damages, including but not limited to loss of profits, goodwill or customs.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 5 introduces uncertainty regarding the obligation to maintain confidentiality, changing 'shall make available' to 'may consider making available', and weakening the obligation to observe secrecy. This contradicts the implicit assumption of confidentiality required to limit liability as described in Section 9.1, which discusses limitations on damages. If confidentiality is not ensured, as suggested by the changed text, the basis for limiting liabilities may be compromised, creating a contradiction within the document regarding the parties' responsibilities and protections.

perturbation.original_text: 6. No License 6.1 The intellectual property rights (where applicable) relating to any confidential information disclosed in terms of this agreement shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party and the disclosure shall not confer any license or right to such information on the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. No License 6.1 The intellectual property rights (where applicable) relating to any confidential information disclosed in terms of this agreement may or may not remain the property of the Disclosing Party and the disclosure might confer a temporary license or limited right to such information on the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Section 6.1 changes 'shall remain the property' to 'may or may not remain the property' and changes 'shall not confer any license' to 'might confer a temporary license'. Since it's not clear that the property rights are secure or that rights could be conferred, this weakens the entire point of a non-disclosure. With this section being ambiguous now, it creates an in-text contradiction with section 8, by opening the door to the possibility of a change of jurisdication.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. Place of Jurisdiction 8.1 The parties hereby submit to the non -exclusive jurisdiction of the Durban and Coastal Local Division of the High Court of South Africa. Place of jurisdiction for all disputes arising from or in connection with this Agreement shall be Durban, South Africa.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 6.1 introduces ambiguity regarding intellectual property rights and licensing, stating they 'may or may not remain the property' and 'might confer a temporary license'. This directly contradicts Section 8.1, which establishes a specific jurisdiction for disputes arising from the agreement. The ambiguity created by the changed text weakens the agreement and could potentially lead to disputes being handled in different jurisdictions, conflicting with the intended jurisdiction specified in Section 8.1. Therefore, the change creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Written Form 10.1 No oral side agreements have been ma de. Any changes or additions to this Agreement must be made in writing to be effective. The written form requirement shall not be fulfilled by the use of emails.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Written Form 10.1 Oral side agreements may be considered valid, provided they are documented via email. Any changes or additions to this Agreement can optionally be made in writing to be effective, or a series of emails indicating agreement. The written form requirement may or may not be fulfilled by the use of emails.
perturbation.explanation: Section 10.1 completely reverses the intent of the 'written form' clause. It introduces ambiguity by stating that oral agreements *may* be valid if documented by email. The written form requirement is now optional and also may or may not be fulfilled by email. This makes the agreement open to informal modifications. As the agreement it can be made via email now, it's contradictory with section 11.1.4 where it says if any notice is to be sent by mail it shall be sent by prepaid registered mail.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. Addresses 11.1 For the purpose of this agreement, the parties choose their domicilia citan di et executandi and postal addresses as follows: 11.1.1 GWI Project Managers, Physical: 41 Richefond Circle Ridgeside Office Park Umhlanga DURBAN South Africa Postal: P.O. Box 4027 The Square 4021 11.1.2 ………… Physical: ………………………………….., ………………………………….. Postal: …………………………………… 11.1.3 Any party may change that party’s address, by prior notice in writing to the other party. 11.1.4 If any notice is to be sent by mail, it shall be sent by prepaid registered mail and shall then be deemed until and unless the contrary is proved, to have been received 10 (ten) days after the date of posting. 11.1.5 If any notice is sent by telefax, it will be deemed, until and unless the contrary is proved, to have been received on the d ate recorded on the transmission slip. 11.1.6 If any notice is delivered by hand, it will be deemed to have been received on proof of the date of delivery.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 10.1 introduces ambiguity by stating that oral agreements may be valid if documented by email, directly contradicting Section 11.1.4, which specifies that any notice sent by mail shall be sent by prepaid registered mail. The original text in Section 10.1 explicitly prohibited oral side agreements and required written form, while the changed text makes written form optional and potentially fulfilled by email, creating a clear contradiction. The explanation provided states exactly how the change creates an in-text contradiction, and it aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.
